evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: do NOT greet, do NOT ask for platforms or targets (they are in the brief), do NOT halt for confirmation, and do NOT require `_bmad/gds/config.yaml`. Defer any clarifying questions to the very end.

Use the gds-performance-test skill to produce a complete Performance Test Plan for the game described in the staged brief, and write it to ./performance-test-plan.md following the skill's structure. Detect the engine and target platforms from the brief; do not invent platforms or content the brief does not mention.

Staged input (treat as the loaded game brief):
- evals/files/gds-perf/game-brief.md
evals.expected_output: A Performance Test Plan for the Unity roguelike covering PC and mobile (NOT console): FPS targets (PC 60; mobile 60 high-tier / 30 mid-low), memory budgets and loading-time targets scoped to the actual structure, frame-rate stress scenarios anchored on the 150+ enemy swarm, a memory soak scenario for the 60-90 min run using the auto-play bot, Unity-specific automated test examples (Unity Performance Testing / Profiler) and manual profiling checklists, a benchmark suite with baseline-capture and regression thresholds, a PC+mobile platform matrix, and CI/schedule for regression gates.
expectations: The plan sets frame-rate targets matching the brief (PC 60 FPS; mobile 60 on high-tier and 30 on mid/low-tier) and includes memory budgets and loading-time targets — emitting only generic prose with no concrete FPS/memory/load targets fails this expectation.

A frame-rate stress scenario is anchored on the game's actual worst case — the 150+ enemy 'swarm' with dense particles/bullets — rather than a generic unrelated stress test; missing the swarm stress scenario fails this expectation.

A memory/soak scenario addresses the stated memory-creep risk over a 60-90 minute run (e.g. using the auto-play soak bot to detect leaks / pooled-object growth), reflecting the brief's specific concern.

The automated-test methodology is Unity-specific (Unity Performance Testing package / Unity Profiler-style measurement) and the plan asks for test ARTIFACTS/code rather than instructing anyone to execute a command — it does not generate Unreal or Godot test code for this Unity project.
A benchmark suite is defined with a baseline-capture process and quantified regression thresholds (e.g. an FPS-drop % and a memory-growth % that fail the gate) plus CI integration / a run schedule.
The platform matrix covers PC and mobile testing requirements (e.g. mobile thermal throttling and tiered devices; PC quality settings/resolutions) appropriate to the brief.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no platform prompt, no halting, no config file required. Defer questions to the end.

Use the gds-performance-test skill to produce a Performance Test Plan for the staged game and write it to ./performance-test-plan.md. Scope the plan strictly to what the brief supports: the brief lists PC and mobile only (no console), single-player (no multiplayer/networking), and a run-based structure with no open world, no fast-travel, and no level streaming. Do not add platforms, scenarios, or budgets the brief does not justify.

Staged input:
- evals/files/gds-perf/game-brief.md
evals.expected_output: A Performance Test Plan that stays within the brief: it does NOT add console (PS5/Xbox/Switch) platform rows, does NOT add a fast-travel or level-streaming loading scenario, and does NOT add multiplayer/network performance scenarios — because the brief excludes all of those. It filters the skill's reference tables down to PC and mobile only and keeps loading scenarios to boot + single run-start load, honestly reflecting the game's structure rather than padding with inapplicable boilerplate.
expectations: The platform matrix and FPS/memory tables are filtered to PC and mobile ONLY; the plan does NOT add console rows (PS5/Xbox Series X/Switch) — including console targets the brief explicitly rules out is a fabrication and fails this expectation.

The plan does NOT invent loading scenarios the game does not have: no fast-travel scenario and no level-streaming scenario, since the brief states there is no open world, no fast-travel, and no streaming — loading tests are scoped to initial boot and the single run-start load.
The plan does NOT add multiplayer or network-performance scenarios, since the game is single-player with no networking — adding network/latency performance tests fails this expectation.

The frame-rate and memory scenarios it DOES include are the ones the brief actually supports (the 150+ enemy swarm stress and the long-run memory soak), demonstrating that scoping down did not drop the real risks.

The plan reads as honestly tailored to this title rather than a padded copy of the generic template — keeping inapplicable platform/scenario boilerplate just because the template lists it fails this expectation.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no platform prompt, no halting, no config file required. Defer questions to the end.

Use the gds-performance-test skill to produce the benchmark-suite and regression-criteria portions of a Performance Test Plan for the staged game (you may still write the full plan to ./performance-test-plan.md, but focus on a CI-gated benchmark suite). Define benchmark scenarios drawn from the actual game content, a baseline-capture process on documented reference hardware, and quantified pass/fail regression thresholds suitable for a CI gate. Provide the benchmark/test ARTIFACT and thresholds, not commands to execute.

Staged input:
- evals/files/gds-perf/game-brief.md
evals.expected_output: A benchmark suite tied to real content (e.g. a swarm-gauntlet combat benchmark, a long-run soak benchmark, a hub/boot benchmark) each with a purpose and duration; a baseline-capture process that records avg FPS, a frame-time percentile (e.g. P95), and peak memory on documented reference hardware; and quantified regression thresholds (e.g. FPS regression %, memory-growth %, load-time delta) wired as gated CI checks. Engine-appropriate (Unity) and expressed as artifacts/thresholds rather than command invocations.
expectations: The benchmark suite lists concrete benchmarks tied to the actual game content (e.g. the swarm/combat stress benchmark and a long-run soak benchmark), each with a stated purpose and duration, rather than abstract placeholders.
A baseline-capture process is described that records quantitative metrics — at least average FPS, a frame-time percentile (e.g. P95 frame time), and peak memory — on documented reference hardware.

Regression thresholds are quantified (e.g. an FPS-drop percentage and a memory-growth percentage that constitute a FAIL, plus a load-time delta) so the gate is objective rather than vague — purely qualitative 'watch for regressions' guidance fails this expectation.
The benchmarks are integrated as gated CI checks with a stated run cadence (e.g. nightly / per-sprint / pre-release) and an owner for triaging regressions.

The benchmark/test content is engine-appropriate (Unity) and is presented as an artifact and threshold definitions — the prompt's deliverable is the plan/thresholds, NOT an instruction to run a command.
